title: The Spark
subtitle: Something from nothing

briefing.narrative:
You arrive at the Monastery of Version Control on a rainy Tuesday. The Head Monk, a weathered figure who claims to have invented the semicolon, blocks the gate.

"Before you may enter," he intones, "you must prove you can create Something From Nothing. Initialize a repository, write a file, and commit it. The universe began with less."

He pauses. "Also, the kitchen desperately needs a grocery list. Long story. Don't ask."
briefing.concept: git init creates a new Git repository in the current directory. Think of it as founding a monastery — nothing is tracked until someone takes the first step. git add stages a file, marking it for inclusion in the next commit. git commit saves a permanent snapshot of everything that's staged.
objectives: Initialize a new git repository with 'git init'
Create a file (any name, any content)

tips: Type 'help' anytime to see available commands
Made a mistake? Click the UNDO button or type 'undo'

hints.text: The Head Monk leans in conspiratorially: 'Start with git init. Then create a file. Any file. echo "hello" > hello.txt works fine.'
A passing monk drops a scroll at your feet: 'git add <file> stages it. git commit -m "message" saves it. That is the way.'
The monastery cat walks across your keyboard and somehow types: git init && echo "hello" > hello.txt && git add hello.txt && git commit -m "First commit"
